main_paper.raw: {"methods":["ATOMFLOW: a deep generative model based on flow-matching for ligand-binding protein design from 2D molecular graphs","Unified biotoken representation for proteins and molecules, operating on representative atoms","SE(3)-equivariant structure prediction network for learning the flow vector field","Rectified flow approach for generative modeling at the atomic level","Variant of Frame Aligned Point Error (FAPE) loss adapted for multi-scale geometric features"],"problems":["Designing novel proteins that bind to small molecules without requiring known bound ligand conformations","Capturing ligand flexibility and protein-ligand interactions in protein design","Reducing computational cost and assumptions compared to methods that require fixed ligand conformers or full protein scaffolds"],"datasets":["Subset of structures from the Protein Data Bank (PDB)"],"metrics":["Self-consistency","Binding affinity","Diversity","Novelty"],"results":[{"metric":"Overall performance compared to RFDiffusionAA","value":"Matches state-of-the-art performance"},{"metric":"Binding affinity (when bound structure is unknown)","value":"ATOMFLOW successfully designs high-affinity binders, outperforming RFDiffusionAA in this scenario"}],"novelty_claims":["First deep generative model for ligand-binding protein design that operates directly from 2D molecular graphs without requiring bound ligand structures","Unified biotoken framework for modeling proteins and molecules at the atomic level in a shared spatial representation","Directly models and updates both ligand and protein structures iteratively, capturing ligand flexibility","SE(3)-equivariant flow-matching approach for protein-ligand complex generation","Versatile framework potentially applicable to various biomolecule generation tasks"]}

methods: ATOMFLOW: a deep generative model based on flow-matching for ligand-binding protein design from 2D molecular graphs
Unified biotoken representation for proteins and molecules, operating on representative atoms
SE(3)-equivariant structure prediction network for learning the flow vector field
Rectified flow approach for generative modeling at the atomic level
Variant of Frame Aligned Point Error (FAPE) loss adapted for multi-scale geometric features
problems: Designing novel proteins that bind to small molecules without requiring known bound ligand conformations
Capturing ligand flexibility and protein-ligand interactions in protein design
Reducing computational cost and assumptions compared to methods that require fixed ligand conformers or full protein scaffolds
datasets: Subset of structures from the Protein Data Bank (PDB)

results.value: Matches state-of-the-art performance

results.metric: Binding affinity (when bound structure is unknown)
results.value: ATOMFLOW successfully designs high-affinity binders, outperforming RFDiffusionAA in this scenario

novelty_claims: First deep generative model for ligand-binding protein design that operates directly from 2D molecular graphs without requiring bound ligand structures
Unified biotoken framework for modeling proteins and molecules at the atomic level in a shared spatial representation
Directly models and updates both ligand and protein structures iteratively, capturing ligand flexibility
SE(3)-equivariant flow-matching approach for protein-ligand complex generation
Versatile framework potentially applicable to various biomolecule generation tasks

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["HarmonicFlow: a generative process for 3D protein-ligand binding structures using self-conditioned flow matching","FlowSite: an extension of HarmonicFlow to jointly generate protein pocket residue types and ligand 3D binding structure","Self-conditioned flow matching objective for training generative models","Fake-ligand data augmentation (treating side chains as ligands)"],"problems":["Designing protein binding pockets for small molecules (ligands) without prior knowledge of ligand 3D structure or binding pose","Jointly generating protein pocket residue identities and ligand 3D binding poses","Designing binding sites that can bind multiple ligands and ions (multi-ligand binding)","Improving generative models for protein-ligand docking"],"datasets":["PDBBind"],"metrics":["Binding site amino acid recovery (%)","Average sample quality (for docking tasks)"],"results":[{"metric":"Binding site amino acid recovery (FlowSite)","value":"47.0%"},{"metric":"Binding site amino acid recovery (baseline)","value":"39.4%"},{"metric":"Binding site amino acid recovery (oracle with ground truth ligand pose)","value":"51.4%"},{"metric":"Average sample quality (docking, HarmonicFlow vs. product-space diffusion)","value":"HarmonicFlow outperforms product-space diffusion on multiple pocket-level docking tasks"}],"novelty_claims":["FlowSite is the first deep learning solution for designing binding sites for small molecules without prior knowledge of the molecule structure.","FlowSite provides a general and automated approach to binding site design, in contrast to previous methods that require expert knowledge and manual steps.","HarmonicFlow is a simpler and more general generative process for 3D ligand binding structures, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in average sample quality and applicability.","FlowSite is the first general solution for multi-ligand binding site design."]}

methods: HarmonicFlow: a generative process for 3D protein-ligand binding structures using self-conditioned flow matching
FlowSite: an extension of HarmonicFlow to jointly generate protein pocket residue types and ligand 3D binding structure
Self-conditioned flow matching objective for training generative models
Fake-ligand data augmentation (treating side chains as ligands)
problems: Designing protein binding pockets for small molecules (ligands) without prior knowledge of ligand 3D structure or binding pose
Jointly generating protein pocket residue identities and ligand 3D binding poses
Designing binding sites that can bind multiple ligands and ions (multi-ligand binding)
Improving generative models for protein-ligand docking
datasets: PDBBind

results.metric: Binding site amino acid recovery (oracle with ground truth ligand pose)
Average sample quality (docking, HarmonicFlow vs. product-space diffusion)
results.value: HarmonicFlow outperforms product-space diffusion on multiple pocket-level docking tasks

novelty_claims: FlowSite is the first deep learning solution for designing binding sites for small molecules without prior knowledge of the molecule structure.
FlowSite provides a general and automated approach to binding site design, in contrast to previous methods that require expert knowledge and manual steps.
HarmonicFlow is a simpler and more general generative process for 3D ligand binding structures, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in average sample quality and applicability.
FlowSite is the first general solution for multi-ligand binding site design.

methods: RoseTTAFold All-Atom (RFAA): a deep network for modeling full biological assemblies including proteins, nucleic acids, small molecules, metals, and covalent modifications
RFdiffusion All-Atom (RFdiffusionAA): a fine-tuned version of RFAA for generative design, building protein structures around small molecules using diffusive denoising tasks
problems: Inability of existing methods (e.g., AlphaFold2, RoseTTAFold) to model covalent modifications or interactions with small molecules and non-protein molecules
Lack of methods for simultaneous modeling of assemblies containing proteins, nucleic acids, small molecules, and covalent modifications
Need for generative design of proteins that bind to small molecules and non-protein molecules
datasets: Protein Data Bank (PDB) structures of full biological assemblies
metrics: Performance in CAMEO for flexible backbone small molecule docking
Prediction accuracy for protein covalent modifications and assemblies

results.metric: Protein structure prediction accuracy (RFAA vs AF2)
results.value: comparable

results.metric: Performance in CAMEO for flexible backbone small molecule docking
results.value: excellent

results.metric: Prediction accuracy for protein covalent modifications and assemblies with nucleic acids and small molecules
results.value: reasonable

novelty_claims: RFAA is the first deep network capable of modeling full biological assemblies with proteins, nucleic acids, small molecules, metals, and covalent modifications simultaneously.
RFAA achieves comparable accuracy to AF2 for protein structure prediction and outperforms in flexible backbone small molecule docking.
RFdiffusionAA enables generative design of protein binding pockets around small molecules and non-protein molecules, which was not possible with previous methods.
Experimental validation of designed proteins binding to therapeutically and functionally relevant small molecules (digoxigenin, heme, bilin).

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["RoseTTAFold diffusion (RFdiffusion): fine-tuning the RoseTTAFold structure prediction network on protein structure denoising tasks to create a generative model for protein backbones"],"problems":["Lack of a general deep-learning framework for de novo protein design that can address a wide range of design challenges","Difficulty in applying diffusion models to protein modelling due to complex backbone geometry and sequence–structure relationships","Need for effective methods for de novo binder design, higher-order symmetric architecture design, enzyme active site scaffolding, and symmetric motif scaffolding"],"datasets":["Experimentally characterized designed symmetric assemblies","Experimentally characterized designed metal-binding proteins","Experimentally characterized designed protein binders"],"metrics":["Structural accuracy (e.g., comparison to cryo-EM structure)","Functional validation (experimental characterization)"],"results":[{"metric":"Structural accuracy (cryo-EM structure similarity)","value":"Designed binder in complex with influenza haemagglutinin is nearly identical to the design model"},{"metric":"Experimental validation","value":"Hundreds of designed symmetric assemblies, metal-binding proteins, and protein binders were experimentally characterized for structure and function"}],"novelty_claims":["First general deep-learning framework for protein design that enables solution of a wide range of design challenges, including de novo binder design and higher-order symmetric architectures","Successful application of diffusion models to protein backbone generation by fine-tuning on denoising tasks","RFdiffusion enables design of diverse functional proteins from simple molecular specifications, analogous to image generation from user-specified inputs"]}

methods: RoseTTAFold diffusion (RFdiffusion): fine-tuning the RoseTTAFold structure prediction network on protein structure denoising tasks to create a generative model for protein backbones
problems: Lack of a general deep-learning framework for de novo protein design that can address a wide range of design challenges
Difficulty in applying diffusion models to protein modelling due to complex backbone geometry and sequence–structure relationships
Need for effective methods for de novo binder design, higher-order symmetric architecture design, enzyme active site scaffolding, and symmetric motif scaffolding
datasets: Experimentally characterized designed symmetric assemblies
Experimentally characterized designed metal-binding proteins
Experimentally characterized designed protein binders
metrics: Structural accuracy (e.g., comparison to cryo-EM structure)

results.value: Designed binder in complex with influenza haemagglutinin is nearly identical to the design model
Hundreds of designed symmetric assemblies, metal-binding proteins, and protein binders were experimentally characterized for structure and function

novelty_claims: First general deep-learning framework for protein design that enables solution of a wide range of design challenges, including de novo binder design and higher-order symmetric architectures
Successful application of diffusion models to protein backbone generation by fine-tuning on denoising tasks
RFdiffusion enables design of diverse functional proteins from simple molecular specifications, analogous to image generation from user-specified inputs

methods: Flow-based generative modeling for protein conformational landscapes
Repurposing AlphaFold and ESMFold under a custom flow matching framework (AlphaFlow and ESMFlow)
Fine-tuning on PDB structures and molecular dynamics ensembles (ATLAS dataset)
Scale-invariant noising process for flow matching
problems: Generating protein structural ensembles rather than single static structures
Capturing conformational heterogeneity and flexibility in protein structure prediction
Generalizing ensemble generation to protein language model-based predictors (e.g., ESMFold)
Learning from and replicating ensembles beyond the PDB, such as those from molecular dynamics simulations
Providing a computationally efficient proxy for expensive physics-based simulations
datasets: PDB (Protein Data Bank)
ATLAS dataset (molecular dynamics simulations)
metrics: Replication of higher-order ensemble observables (e.g., intermittent contacts, solvent exposure)

results.value: AlphaFlow and ESMFlow substantially surpass MSA ablation baselines on a test set of conformationally heterogeneous proteins
AlphaFlow surpasses MSA baselines on test proteins structurally dissimilar from the training set
AlphaFlow achieves faster convergence than replicate MD trajectories when diversifying a static PDB structure

novelty_claims: First application of flow matching to repurpose AlphaFold and ESMFold as sequence-conditioned generative models for protein structure ensembles
Custom flow matching framework tailored to protein structure prediction, including a scale-invariant noising process resilient to missing/cropped residues
Ability to train and sample from arbitrary ensembles, including those from molecular dynamics, not just PDB
Generalizes ensemble generation to PLM-based predictors (e.g., ESMFold), not just MSA-based methods
Provides a computationally efficient alternative to expensive molecular dynamics simulations for generating protein ensembles

methods: Genie: a generative model of protein structures using denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) with SE(3)-equivariant neural networks and Invariant Point Attention (IPA) for geometric reasoning
Discrete-time diffusion over a cloud of oriented residue reference frames in 3D space, with geometric asymmetry: point clouds in the forward process and reference frames in the reverse process
problems: Generating novel, designable, and diverse protein structures for protein design
Overcoming limitations of search-based and sampling-based methods in exploring the vast space of protein structures
Ensuring generated protein structures are physically realizable (designable) and not just mathematically plausible
Capturing SE(3)-equivariance and chemical chirality in generative models for protein structures
datasets: Not explicitly stated in the provided content, but likely uses datasets of natural protein structures (e.g., PDB) for training and evaluation
metrics: Designability (likelihood that a structure can be realized by a protein sequence)
Diversity (coverage of a wide range of geometries)

results.value: Genie generates protein backbones that are more designable than existing models
Genie generates structures that are more novel (dissimilar to natural proteins) than existing models
Genie generates structures that are more diverse than existing models

novelty_claims: Genie introduces a geometric asymmetry in the diffusion process: using point clouds for noising and reference frames for generation, which is novel compared to prior work.
Genie achieves state-of-the-art performance on key design metrics (designability, novelty, diversity) compared to existing generative models for protein structure.
Genie leverages SE(3)-equivariant reasoning and IPA in a diffusion model for unconditional protein structure generation, without violating the Gaussian assumption of DDPMs.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Dynamic Multi-channel Equivariant Graph Network (dyMEAN): an end-to-end, full-atom, E(3)-equivariant model for antibody design","Structural initialization based on conserved residues for knowledgeable guess of antibody structure","Shadow paratope: a novel representation to bridge epitope-antibody connections and enable invariant interaction modeling","Adaptive multi-channel equivariant encoder for iterative update of 1D sequence and 3D structure, handling variable residue sizes and full-atom geometry","Epitope-antibody docking via alignment of the shadow paratope"],"problems":["Current learning-based antibody design methods only address subtasks, leading to suboptimal or resource-intensive solutions","Existing models omit framework regions or side chains, failing to capture full-atom geometry","Lack of an end-to-end computational solution for antibody design given only the epitope and incomplete antibody sequence","Inefficiency and incompleteness in current full-atom modeling approaches, especially for the entire antibody"],"datasets":["Epitope-binding CDR-H3 design datasets (specific datasets not named in the provided text)","Datasets for complex structure prediction (not specifically named)","Datasets for affinity optimization (not specifically named)"],"metrics":["Epitope-binding CDR-H3 design accuracy","Complex structure prediction accuracy","Affinity optimization performance"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["First end-to-end, full-atom, E(3)-equivariant model for antibody design given only the epitope and incomplete antibody sequence","Introduction of shadow paratope to model antigen-antibody interactions invariant to initial orientation and position","Adaptive multi-channel message passing to handle variable atom numbers in residues for full-atom geometry","Directly addresses the global antibody design problem, unlike previous multi-stage or partial solutions"]}

methods: Dynamic Multi-channel Equivariant Graph Network (dyMEAN): an end-to-end, full-atom, E(3)-equivariant model for antibody design
Structural initialization based on conserved residues for knowledgeable guess of antibody structure
Shadow paratope: a novel representation to bridge epitope-antibody connections and enable invariant interaction modeling

Epitope-antibody docking via alignment of the shadow paratope
problems: Current learning-based antibody design methods only address subtasks, leading to suboptimal or resource-intensive solutions
Existing models omit framework regions or side chains, failing to capture full-atom geometry

Inefficiency and incompleteness in current full-atom modeling approaches, especially for the entire antibody

Adaptive multi-channel message passing to handle variable atom numbers in residues for full-atom geometry
Directly addresses the global antibody design problem, unlike previous multi-stage or partial solutions

methods: FoldingDiff: a diffusion-based generative model for protein backbone structure generation using an internal angle representation and a vanilla transformer architecture
Internal angle representation of protein backbones (using 3 bond and 3 dihedral angles per residue) to achieve rotation and translation invariance
Denoising diffusion probabilistic modeling with wrapped Gaussian noise for periodic angles
Bidirectional transformer as the denoising network
Baselines: autoregressive transformer model using the same angle representation, and a random angle shuffling baseline
problems: Direct generation of diverse, novel, and physically foldable protein structures from neural networks
Overcoming limitations of previous generative models that require post-processing or complex equivariant architectures
Ensuring generated protein structures are realistic, designable, and diverse
datasets: CATH protein domain dataset (de-duplicated, 30,395 unique protein domains, 40-128 residues)
metrics: Distributional similarity of generated angles to natural proteins (including Ramachandran plots)
Secondary structure content and co-occurrence (using P-SEA annotation)
Self-consistency TM score (scTM) for designability (using ProteinMPNN for inverse folding and OmegaFold for structure prediction)
Maximum TM score to training set (novelty)

results.value: 22.7% (177/780) of generated structures are designable

results.metric: Designability of random angle shuffling baseline (scTM ≥ 0.5)

results.value: 89% (but only produces singular alpha helices, not diverse structures)
-0.38 (longer structures are less designable)

results.metric: Spearman correlation (training TM score vs. scTM)

results.value: FoldingDiff's designable backbones are as diverse as natural proteins and more diverse than prior methods or baselines

novelty_claims: First diffusion-based generative model for protein backbone structures that operates directly on internal angles, removing the need for complex equivariant neural networks
Internal angle representation provides intrinsic rotation and translation invariance, simplifying the model architecture and improving robustness
FoldingDiff generates protein backbones with realistic, diverse, and designable structures, including multiple secondary structure elements, not just simple motifs like alpha helices
Demonstrates that a simple transformer-based diffusion model can match or exceed the diversity and designability of more complex or heavily post-processed generative models
Open-source codebase and trained models released for protein structure diffusion
